❖ ❖ ❖ ฟ้อน The Dance of the Data A narrative tour of the village · LannaBench Codenames
❖

Once, in a village below the Phu Chi Fah, a ปู่จ๋านPu Jan — a lay minister and ceremonial leader, keeper of Buddhist scripture and the Tai Tham script, who serves as the bridge between the temple and the villagers — sat in the ข่วงKhuang — the open courtyard in front of the temple. Before him lay a pile of raw bamboo, a stack of palm leaves, and a lacquered อูบOob — a fine lacquerware vessel of woven bamboo, gilded in the Tai Khoen tradition — containing sacred ข้าวตอกKhao Tok.

He began to speak to his granddaughter, who would one day carry the old ways into the new world of machines.

· · ·

"Child," he said, "to build something true, you must first gather water from the mountain stream. Our น้ำต้นNam Ton cools the water naturally, as it has for hundreds of years. That is your first task: take the raw words of Lanna people, and pour them into a clean vessel."

He pointed to the loom in the corner, its wooden frame standing silent.

"That is your หูกHuuk. Every thread must pass through it — every sentence must be checked. One wrong thread, and the whole pattern fails."

Then he took a knife and split a length of bamboo into thin, even strips — ตอกTok.

"You will cut the data into parts: one for learning, one for testing, one for proof. The cut must be clean. No leaks."

He dipped a brush into a pot of black รักRak — lacquer from the rak tree.

"Now coat each word with meaning. Intent, tone, construction. This is the รัก that preserves our speech. Each layer of lacquer is a layer of understanding."

He pointed to the house across the courtyard, where a pair of carved wooden planks crossed above the gable — กาแลKalae — standing watch over the roofline.

"The กาแล watches. It will catch the failures, the flips in tone, the constructions that fall apart. It protects the house."

· · ·

Then he led her into the ครัวไฟKrua Fai — the traditional kitchen, where smoke and heat were the heart of every home.

"Here is the ไหข้าวHai Khao," he said, placing a cylindrical carved teak steamer over a boiling pot. "You will steam your prompts here — soak the template, fill it with examples, let the steam rise evenly around the words."

He pointed to a clay stove glowing with charcoal — อั้งโล่Ang Lo.

"That is the slow fire. When you fine-tune a model, you do not roar. You simmer. Low heat, long time. The อั้งโล่ knows patience."

Beside the stove lay a wide wooden tray — กัวะKua — still warm from the morning's sticky rice.

"When the rice is cooked, you toss it here. Fast, light, cool. That is inference: a single toss, and the words are ready."

· · ·

Finally, he led her to the temple itself. At the top of the roof, a graceful wooden finial — จ่อฟ้าJor Fa — arched toward the sky.

"That is where you measure. The จ่อฟ้า connects earth to heaven. You will send your outputs up there, and you will count the distance between what was said and what should have been said. Use the แก้วKaew — the crystal — to split the light into three numbers: the best, the average, the difference. The three scores are like the three realms — ไตรภูมิTrai Phum: heaven, earth, and the world below. Use the ป่องPong to see the gap between the mountain and the plain."

She looked at the sand pagoda, where a small ตุงTung — a ritual flag — flapped in the wind.

"That flag marks our intentions," the old man said. "Every field, every value, every definition. It never moves from its place."

· · ·

In his hand, he held a palm-leaf manuscript — ใบลานBai Lan — fresh and empty.

"This is for the humans. They will write their judgments here, each mark a grain of rice offered to the festival."

He gestured toward the centre of the village, where a ฮอมปอยHom Poi was being prepared — neighbours bringing baskets of rice, jars of soup, bundles of sweets, all for a single feast.

"That is how we measure agreement. Not by counting silences, but by watching the community carry their offerings together. One feast, one voice."

· · ·

Finally, he picked up a handful of ข้าวตอกKhao Tok and tossed it into the air. The grains scattered, then landed in a perfect circle around the young girl's feet.

"Every experiment begins with a seed. A single number, a fixed starting point. From one grain, a thousand blooms. That is the ฟ้อนFon — the dance of the data, layer by layer, until the whole story is told."

· · ·

She bowed, and the old man smiled.

"Now go. Run the dance. Let the world see our words — not as a sample, but as a living language, preserved in code and carried forward by the wind."
